ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301415 covers eggplant food product as a closest parent; no specific code exists for tender/young Chinese-style cooked eggplant dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers aubergines/eggplants as a raw commodity; no specific code for the cooked Chinese tender eggplant dish preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 identifies eggplant as a substance; used as closest parent since no code exists for the prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of tender purple eggplant is approximately 200 g, representing a shared vegetable side dish for one to two persons.

nutrient_source.title: Eggplant, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169228)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy cooked eggplant (boiled, drained, no salt) used as the primary nutrient base. Values adjusted upward for sodium to reflect typical Chinese restaurant preparation with soy sauce seasoning (estimated +80 mg sodium per 100 g). All other macros and micronutrients retained from USDA SR Legacy cooked eggplant values. This is the closest defensible approved-source match for a cooked Chinese eggplant dish.

gi_source.title: Eggplant — Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load values from the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Eggplant GI is reported as approximately 15 (low GI) in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). This value is applied to the cooked Chinese eggplant dish as the closest defensible analogous food. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 8.64 × 2.0) / 100 = 2.6, confirming very low GL.

hypertension.guidance: Chinese-style tender eggplant dishes are typically seasoned with soy sauce, contributing approximately 163 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium preparation or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 15 and 2.5 g dietary fibre per 100 g, tender eggplant produces a minimal postprandial glucose response and is a suitable vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and antioxidant nasunin content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tender eggplant is very low in saturated fat (0.04 g per 100 g) and contains zero cholesterol; its anthocyanin content (nasunin) has demonstrated antioxidant properties in peer-reviewed literature, supporting cardiovascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tender Purple Eggplant (嫩茄子, Nèn Qiézi) is a classic Chinese vegetable dish featuring young, thin-skinned purple eggplant that is prized for its soft, silky texture and mild, slightly sweet flavour. In Chinese cuisine, the dish is most commonly prepared by steaming or braising the eggplant until tender, then dressing it with a savoury sauce of garlic, soy sauce, sesame oil, and sometimes chilli or oyster sauce. The result is a dish with a melt-in-the-mouth texture, a rich umami-garlic aroma, and a glossy, deeply flavoured sauce. Nutritionally, eggplant is very low in calories (approximately 35 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and provides modest dietary fibre (2.5 g per 100 g) and carbohydrate (8.6 g per 100 g). Its glycemic index is low (approximately 15), making it an excellent vegetable choice for individuals managing blood glucose levels, including those with Type 2 Diabetes. The primary nutritional concern in restaurant preparations is sodium from soy sauce seasoning, which can reach 163 mg per 100 g. Eggplant contains the anthocyanin nasunin, a potent antioxidant found in the purple skin, which has been associated with cardiovascular and neuroprotective benefits in research literature. The dish is naturally vegan, halal, and dairy-free, though it contains soy allergens from the sauce. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a shared vegetable side in Chinese zi char restaurants and is known by the Malay name Terung Ungu Lembut and the Tamil name மென்மையான கத்திரிக்காய்.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chinese eggplant dish?
How is Chinese steamed eggplant prepared?
Is brinjal good for blood pressure?
What is the glycemic load of eggplant?
meal_context: Chinese zi char restaurant shared vegetable side dish

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked eggplant (FDC ID 169228), which is an approved secondary global source. No exact Singapore-specific or Chinese restaurant-specific nutrient entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this dish. Sodium has been adjusted upward to reflect typical soy sauce seasoning in Chinese restaurant preparation. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database via the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008). Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy cooked eggplant (boiled, drained, no salt; FDC ID 169228) with sodium adjusted to approximately 163 mg per 100 g to reflect typical Chinese restaurant soy sauce seasoning. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; eggplant contains no trans fats by nature (naturally zero). 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients in the base vegetable. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked eggplant; eggplant contains negligible vitamin D (confirmed zero per source). 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.02), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.09) are reported from USDA SR Legacy. 7. GI of 15 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables; applied as analogous estimate for cooked Chinese eggplant dish. 8. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 8.64 × 2.0) / 100 = 2.6. 9. The dish is served at See Ya Tonight 今晚有局 (Singapore) at SGD 20.67. 10. Food group corrected from input 'Mixed' to 'Vegetables' as eggplant is a vegetable dish. 11. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g fields are not present in this record; all micronutrients have been populated from USDA SR Legacy data.
